stories.title: Sweet Memory of her
stories.excerpt: A story of love, loss, and the enduring power of memory. When a young woman discovers a hidden journal in her grandmother's attic, she uncovers a tale of forbidden love and sacrifice that changes everything she thought she knew about her family.
stories.author: Udaya Adhikari

stories.title: The Love I Learned to Believe In
stories.excerpt: Before her, love was just a word. After her, it became faith. A heartfelt journey of patience, friendship, growth, and a love that blossomed from the simplest moments.
stories.author: Udaya Adhikari
stories.fullStory:
This is the story of my life when I was in class 12. It is the story of my first love—the love through which I learned what love truly means. After seeing her, I began to believe in love and trust it. I wasn’t an average student, and I didn’t read many books. Honestly, I never liked reading much. But I was always curious about innovation and new ideas, so I chose science in +2.

One day, while the teacher was teaching, I felt a little bored. I was sitting near the window, watching the flowers outside, birds flying, and students walking around during their break. The teacher kept explaining the lesson, but my attention was somewhere else.

And that’s when I saw her for the first time.

She was walking with her friends—talking, smiling, laughing. The moment I saw her, I froze. It felt like something inside me suddenly stopped. I didn’t even tell my friends about her. From that day on, at the same time every day, I secretly watched her from the classroom window. Some days she came, some days she didn’t—but I waited anyway.

After a week, I found out she was in class 11, also studying science. She was my junior. One girl I knew from school happened to be her friend, so I asked her for her name and Facebook ID. Later, I found her profile. I didn’t have enough courage to talk from my real account, so I created a fake one and started chatting with her.

But I’m not good at lying. Soon, I told her the truth—that I studied in the same college, that I was in class 12, and that I was a science student. I just didn’t tell her my real name.

As we kept talking, she started calling me “Dai.” Every time she said that, I felt uncomfortable. After a few weeks, I finally messaged her from my real account and told her everything. Still, she continued calling me “Dai.” One day, I asked her not to call me that. She asked why and said, “You are my senior, so I will call you Dai.” I didn’t have the courage to tell her I liked her, so I stayed silent.

We talked every day—nothing very special, just small conversations. But they meant everything to me.

Then one day, I couldn’t find her Facebook account. I was terrified. I thought she had blocked me. I even checked from my mother’s account—but still nothing. Later, I realized she had deactivated it. I felt relieved, but now I had no way to talk to her.

So I found another way.

I started going to her class and asking for small things—an apron, a dissection box, a calculator. She always gave them to me without hesitation.

During Dashain and Tihar, I found out it was her birthday. Around the same time, I discovered she had opened a new Facebook account. I sent a friend request, but she didn’t accept it. As her birthday came closer, my heart felt heavier. I had no other way to contact her. On her birthday, I sent a message request, hoping—just hoping—she would see it.

But she didn’t.

After almost a month, college reopened. I had been waiting so long to see her. When I finally saw her again, I couldn’t control myself. Tears rolled down my eyes quietly. She was right there—but still so far away.

Valentine’s Day was coming, and I didn’t know what to do. At first, I thought about proposing in front of everyone. But my courage failed me. The college felt too strict, and fear held me back. So I decided to send her a message instead.

I wrote:

“Thank you for being the reason I smile whenever I see you in college.
I may not have the cutest face,
but my heart is big enough to love you.
Happy Valentine’s Day.
I don’t know why you are ignoring me,
but just once, please respond to my message.”

After everything, she finally replied. She said she wasn’t ready for a relationship. She asked me to stay as a friend and said, “Let’s leave it to the future—we don’t know what will happen.”

After that, we started talking every day. Our conversations grew longer. Our friendship grew deeper. Slowly, something changed between us.

A few months later, we planned to meet at a restaurant for the first time. I was so excited I couldn’t sleep the night before. I kept thinking about how to make her feel special.

On the day of our meeting, my heart was racing. When I reached the place, I saw her from a distance. She was searching for me. When our eyes met, she waved. I waved back, nervous but happy.

It was the first time I saw her in casual clothes. Her loose hair, her smile, the way her eyes looked at me—I can’t forget it. My hands were shaking when we shook hands. I gave her a red rose and some chocolates. She became so happy that she hugged me without thinking.

That hug changed everything.

We talked, laughed, shared small secrets. After eating, we went for a walk. Our hands touched by accident, and I felt something like electricity run through me. After a while, she gently held my hand. That feeling is still impossible to explain.

Later, while sitting quietly, she told me something important. When I first proposed, she didn’t know me and didn’t like me. But now, she had started liking me—not just for what I said, but for who I was.

That evening felt like the beginning of something new.

After that day, our bond became stronger. Late-night talks, video calls, weekly dates—we built something beautiful together. A few months later, I proposed again. This time, she smiled and said, “I’ve been waiting for this since our first date. You were just a little late.”

Years passed. Our love grew deeper. And after four or five years together, I married the girl I once nervously met with a red rose in my shaking hands.

Now, when I look back at that first handshake, that shy smile, that electric touch—I realize love isn’t about big promises. It’s about small moments, pure intentions, and growing together every day.

She wasn’t just my first love.

She became my forever.
quotes: "Love isn’t about big promises. It’s about small moments, pure intentions, and growing together every day."
"She wasn’t just my first love—she became my forever."
